documents.title: League Calendar and Deadlines
documents.summary: The yearly calendar for season rollover, rookie draft, auction, contract deadline, trade deadline, and playoffs.
documents.body_md:
### . League Calendar
- January:
- Off-Season after completion of the Fantasy Playoffs.
- February:
- Off-Season.
- March:
- New League Season Begins.
- Contracts are rolled forward and updated for the upcoming season.
- The goal is to have the league up and running by March 1st, but this may extend to March 15th depending on the commissioner’s ability to finalize preparations.
- April 30:
- Final Day for Expiring Rookie Extensions.
- May:
- Annual Rookie Draft on the Sunday of Memorial Day Weekend.
- The draft is conducted live and broadcast on Discord.
- It is the single most important event of the league year, with the draft typically starting around 6:00-6:30 PM and lasting approximately 4 hours. Expect 10-15 trades during the event.
- Mandatory League Event.
- July:
- Last Weekend of July marks the start of the Free Agent Auction.
- Minimum of 7 Days of Nominations
- Mandatory League Event.
- 3 Days Prior to FA Auction:
- Auction Roster Lock Date, the deadline for cutting players before the auction.
- August:
- Waivers are available at the completion of the FA Auction.
- September:
- Last Sunday before Week 1 is the deadline for Contract Finalization.
- Start of the Fantasy Season.
- November:
- Kickoff Thanksgiving Trade Deadline.
- December:
- Week 15 of the NFL Season marks the start of the Fantasy Playoffs, lasting 3 weeks.
- Section 3: Roster Management

documents.title: League Overview and Governance
documents.summary: League size, divisional structure, owner expectations, membership, and commissioner authority.
documents.body_md:
### Overview
- The UPS Salary Cap Dynasty League is designed to offer a dynamic and competitive fantasy football experience, combining the strategic depth of salary cap management with the long-term planning required in a dynasty format. This league fosters a community of dedicated owners who are committed to fair play, strategic thinking, and a relentless pursuit of championship glory.

### League Structure
- Teams and Divisions:
- The league consists of 12 teams, divided into 4 divisions.
- Divisions are realigned every 3 years based on each team's historical all-play record.
- Division Assignments:
- Division 1: 1/8/9 All-Play
- Division 2: 2/7/10 All-Play
- Division 3: 3/6/11 All-Play
- Division 4: 4/5/12 All-Play
- New owners entering the league during a realignment year are assigned an All-Play record of .500 and then slotted accordingly. If multiple new owners enter the league simultaneously, a random generator will be used to determine the tiebreaker for All-Play assignment.
- Owners taking over a roster mid-season or after the Free Agent Auction has begun will not have that season's record count toward All-Play.
- Future Realignment Years: 2026, 2029, 2032, 2035, etc.
- Playoff Format:
- The league features two sets of playoffs: the UPS Championship and the Hawktuah Bowl (Toilet Bowl).
- Playoff seeding is determined by regular-season performance, with division winners guaranteed a top seed.
- Scoring System:
- The league utilizes a custom scoring system tailored to reward strategic depth and player performance. Details of the scoring system are outlined in [Insert Section Number].

### Expectation of owners
- Owner Expectations:
- All league members are expected to be active participants throughout the season and the off-season. This includes:
- Regular communication via the Discord App.
- Timely responses to trade offers.
- Participation in league events, including the Rookie Draft and Free Agent Auction.
- Maintaining awareness of league rules and important league matters.
- Active Participation:
- Owners are required to field a competitive team for the entire fantasy season, regardless of their standing.
- Actions that attempt to circumvent the competitive balance of the league, such as collusion or other maneuvers, will be grounds for league dismissal.

### League Membership
- Admission Process:
- New members must complete an admission process, including a baseline quiz to ensure a solid understanding of the league's unique structure and value system. This league differs significantly from standard redraft and dynasty formats, requiring time and effort to grasp fully.
- The commissioner will subjectively review the results and present them to the league for approval.
- Probation Period:
- New members are on probation for their first two full seasons.
- The league will assess the new member’s progress in understanding the rules and value. At the end of the probation period, the league will vote on granting full membership.
- Early Termination:
- During probation, the league can vote to remove a new member if they fail to demonstrate sufficient growth in understanding or if their participation jeopardizes league balance.
- Repeated penalties can also result in early termination of membership.
- Dispersal Draft for New Owners:
- When a new owner joins the league, existing teams may choose to throw all of their assets (players and future eligible draft picks, excluding 6th round picks) into a player pool to be combined with the new owner’s assets.
- A random generator will determine the snake draft order, and a draft will take place within Discord for all assets in the pool.
- Once a team commits to the dispersal draft, they cannot withdraw.
- It is crucial that new owners fully understand the league rules before committing, as they will immediately compete with other owners.

### Commissioner's Authority
- Decision-Making:
- The commissioner has full authority on decisions that fall outside the rulebook. In cases where league precedent has been set, this will guide the decision-making process.
- The commissioner may confer with senior league members or expand discussions to the entire league as deemed necessary.
- Best Interest of the League:
- The commissioner always acts in the best interest of the league, ensuring fair play and maintaining competitive balance.

- Our mission is to provide a uniquely kick-ass fantasy football experience that frankly takes a massive dump on any other league ever created. Owners are hand-selected from an elite pool of individuals that have proven on an annual basis that they are dedicated to sacrificing their marriages, career advancement, and any other “life-obstacles” for the betterment of their fantasy football teams and the league itself.

documents.title: Auction Contracts and Loaded Structures
documents.summary: Players acquired in the summer auction, expired rookie auction, or pre-deadline waivers can take multi-year auction contracts, including even, front-loaded, or back-loaded splits.
documents.body_md:
### Eligibility
- Players acquired in the free agent auction, expired rookie auction, or waivers before the contract deadline can receive a multi-year auction contract.
- The owner can choose a 2-year or 3-year deal if they want more control than the default 1-year contract.

### Structure choices
- Even distribution creates a veteran contract with the same salary each year.
- Front-loaded and back-loaded splits are allowed if the total still equals the TCV.
- Back-loaded deals must still satisfy the 20% year-one minimum.

### Default outcome
- If no multi-year auction contract is submitted, the player defaults to a 1-year deal.
- The default type is veteran unless the player is a rookie and later picks up rookie status.

### Cap impact
- Loading more salary into year one can help meet the salary floor.
- Back-loading can reduce current cap pressure but pushes more risk into later seasons.

documents.title: Contract Limits and Compliance Caps
documents.summary: The contract system caps max years, max loaded deals, and max 3-year non-rookie inventory, so owners need to check roster-level compliance before submitting changes.
documents.body_md:
### Roster-level limits
- Maximum contract length for any player is 3 years.
- Maximum 3-year contracts is 6, excluding 3-year rookie contracts in the current written rulebook.
- Maximum loaded contracts is 5 total front-loaded or back-loaded deals.

### Timing rule
- The contract guide also states teams may carry no more than 6 non-rookie contracts from the contract deadline through the end of the season.
- If a proposed transaction would push a team over one of these caps, the owner must resolve that before the move is accepted.

### Compliance check
- Review the roster-wide contract mix before auction submissions, restructures, and trade-linked extensions.
- When a move would solve a salary-floor issue, owners still need the structure to remain compliant with TCV and first-year rules.

documents.title: Contract Primer and Core Terms
documents.summary: The contract system is built around salary, years remaining, TCV, AAV, guarantees, and whether a player is on a rookie, veteran, waiver, or loaded deal.
documents.body_md:
### Definition
- Every UPS contract controls current salary, future cap exposure, and cut liability.
- Owners should think in both yearly salary terms and total contract value before extending or restructuring any player.

### Core terms
- Salary: the player’s current-year salary.
- Contract length: the total number of contract years currently on the player.
- Years remaining: the number of seasons left on the deal.
- TCV: total contract value across the remaining years after any extension or restructure.
- AAV: the evenly distributed yearly value used as the extension baseline.

### Current baseline rules
- Maximum contract length is 3 years at any time.
- Most non-waiver contracts carry a 75% guarantee.
- The first year of any multi-year contract must carry at least 20% of the TCV.

### Why owners care
- These terms drive Contract Command Center calculations, cap planning, trade review, and penalty exposure.

documents.title: Contract Types and Eligibility
documents.summary: UPS distinguishes rookie, veteran, waiver-wire, loaded, auction, and in-season multi-year contracts, and each type changes what an owner can do next.
documents.body_md:
### Rookie contracts
- Rookies drafted in the rookie draft are assigned 3 contract years by default.
- Rookie deals stay evenly distributed and cannot be restructured until they expire into veteran status.
- Rookies picked up on waivers start as short-term pickups and convert to rookie status later if retained.

### Veteran and waiver contracts
- Veteran is the standard contract type for players acquired outside their rookie year.
- WW contracts are the default 1-year contracts for players added after the contract deadline.
- WW contracts can later convert into veteran-style multi-year deals through MYM or extension workflows.

### Loaded contracts
- Front-loaded contracts put more than the AAV into year one.
- Back-loaded contracts put less than the AAV into year one, but year one still must reach the 20% TCV floor.
- Loaded structures are used in auctions and restructures to move cap burden across years without changing TCV.

### Owner impact
- Contract type determines which buttons and previews matter in Contract Command Center and whether current-season salary can change.

documents.title: Contract Extensions
documents.summary: Extensions apply to players entering the final year of their deal and use fixed raise schedules that reset TCV and guarantees for the remaining contract.
documents.body_md:
### Eligibility
- Players entering the final year of their contract can be extended up to the contract deadline date.
- Certain pre-deadline rookie and preseason waiver pickups can be extended by the end of Week 4 if they missed the original deadline and MYM window.
- Players acquired by trade in the final year of their deal must be extended within 4 weeks of acquisition.

### Extension math
- Schedule 1 positions (QB/RB/WR/TE) add $10K for a 1-year extension and $20K for a 2-year extension.
- Schedule 2 positions (DB/LB/DL/K/P) add $3K for a 1-year extension and $5K for a 2-year extension.
- The extension resets TCV and guarantee math to the remaining years after the move.

### Cap impact
- Extensions increase future salary obligations and future penalty exposure.
- Because the new TCV replaces the old one, owners should always review the new guarantee amount before finalizing.

### Workflow rule
- If an extension is attached to a trade, the terms must be documented in trade comments or with proof of discussion before approval.

documents.title: Guarantees, Earned Salary, and Cap Penalties
documents.summary: Most non-waiver contracts are 75% guaranteed, so cut timing and earned-salary milestones matter as much as the raw salary number.
documents.body_md:
### Guarantee baseline
- The majority of contracts carry a 75% TCV guarantee requirement.
- Salary is earned on a month-based schedule: 0% before October, then 25%, 50%, and 75% earned on October 1, November 1, and December 1.

### Exceptions
- 1-year veteran or WW contracts under $5K are cap-free cuts with 0% guarantee.
- Taxi-eligible players carry no guarantee until promotion.
- WW contracts at $5K or more are treated as 65% earned and create a 35% cap penalty if cut before rollover.

### Penalty formula
- Cap penalty is calculated as guaranteed value minus earned salary.
- Penalties after the start of the auction are accrued to the next season, while off-season penalties before the roster lock apply to the current season.

### Practical meaning
- Cutting early protects roster space but usually creates the largest cap hit.
- Keeping a player until more salary is earned can materially reduce the penalty.

documents.title: Legacy Contract Systems and Grandfathered Rules
documents.summary: Older UPS contract eras used different cap-penalty formulas, tag systems, and grandfathered rules, so owners should separate historical memory from the current system.
documents.body_md:
### Historical items owners still remember
- Older rules used a 20% remaining-salary cap-hit model instead of the current guarantee schedule.
- Grandfathered contracts were marked GF until touched by an extension, restructure, or release.
- Legacy franchise and transition tag systems existed in older eras but are not present in the current written rulebook.

### How to treat them now
- Use these items as historical context only unless a commissioner-approved clarification revives or confirms them.
- If an owner believes a legacy exception still applies, it should remain a Needs Confirmation item until resolved.

documents.title: Mid-Year Multi (MYM)
documents.summary: MYM is the in-season retention tool that converts a 1-year contract into a multi-year deal without changing the current-season salary.
documents.body_md:
### What it does
- MYM converts a 1-year contract into a multi-year contract at the same current salary.
- If the contract is a WW deal, MYM converts it into a veteran contract.

### Eligibility and timing
- Owners are allowed a maximum of 3 MYMs per season.
- Players acquired in the auction or preseason waivers who missed pre-season contracts can be given a MYM through the end of NFL Week 2.
- Players acquired in-season on waivers can be given a MYM within 2 weeks of acquisition.

### Restrictions
- MYM deals are not eligible to be loaded.
- The current-season salary may not be modified when the MYM is submitted.

### Why owners use it
- MYM preserves a breakout player without forcing an immediate extension raise into the current season.

documents.title: Restructures and Salary Reallocation
documents.summary: Restructures change how the remaining TCV is distributed across the remaining years without changing what the contract is worth in total.
documents.body_md:
### What triggers a restructure
- Restructures are used in the pre-season for contracts with multiple years remaining.
- The contract guide limits restructures to 3 per season.
- Beginning in 2019, mid-season acquisitions no longer receive restructure allowances.

### Math rules
- A restructure resets the remaining TCV to the new remaining salary plan.
- Owners may split the remaining TCV evenly or create front-loaded or back-loaded years.
- Year one still must satisfy the 20% TCV minimum if the deal is back-loaded.

### Cap impact
- Restructures change when cap pain hits, not how much total cap pain exists.
- A restructure can help address salary-floor, auction, or short-term compliance problems while pushing salary into later seasons.

### Owner caution
- Because guarantee math follows the new remaining contract, restructures can make later cuts more expensive even when they help today.

documents.title: Trade Salary Handling and Extension Language
documents.summary: Trades can include salary movement and extension language, but both must be documented and the teams must remain roster and cap compliant immediately after the deal.
documents.body_md:
### What must be documented
- Trade comments need to show the players, assets, any salary being moved, and any extension terms agreed to as part of the deal.
- Salary-only trades are not allowed; every trade must include an actual asset.

### Compliance timing
- Teams are expected to move back into roster and contract compliance immediately after the trade.
- If the trade creates a contract-compliance issue, the teams have 24 hours to fix it.

### Extension note
- If the outgoing team is agreeing to apply an extension as part of the trade, the terms must be stated in comments or supported with proof of discussion.

### Why owners care
- Trade War Room can combine salary movement with extension planning, so owners need one place to review the combined rule set.

documents.title: 1-Year Extension
documents.summary: The player remains on the roster for two total seasons with a recalculated total contract value of $44K.
documents.body_md:
### Scenario
- Owner wants to extend a Schedule 1 player who is entering the final year of a deal.

### Inputs
- Current salary: $17K
- Years remaining before extension: 1
- Extension term: 1 year
- Extension schedule: Schedule 1

### Calculation
- Added AAV for the new extension year: $10K
- New extension-year salary: $27K
- New TCV after the extension: $44K

### Outcome
- The player remains on the roster for two total seasons with a recalculated total contract value of $44K.

### Why it matters
- This is the baseline extension workflow league owners use most often in Contract Command Center.

documents.summary: The player moves from a one-year expiring deal to a three-year contract with a $40K TCV.
documents.body_md:

### Inputs
- Current salary: $10K
- Years remaining before extension: 1
- Extension term: 2 years
- Extension schedule: Schedule 2

### Calculation
- Added AAV for the new extension years: $5K
- New extension-year salary: $15K
- New TCV after the extension: $40K

### Outcome
- The player moves from a one-year expiring deal to a three-year contract with a $40K TCV.

### Why it matters
- Owners can compare whether a two-year extension is worth the added future cap commitment.

documents.title: Back-Loaded Split
documents.summary: The deal remains valid because year one still meets the minimum 20% of TCV threshold.
documents.body_md:
### Scenario
- Owner wants a three-year back-loaded deal.

### Inputs
- Contract length: 3 years
- Target TCV: $90K
- Year 1 minimum: 20% of TCV

### Calculation
- Minimum year 1 salary: $18K
- Example split: $18K / $32K / $40K
- Total check: $18K + $32K + $40K = $90K

### Outcome
- The deal remains valid because year one still meets the minimum 20% of TCV threshold.

### Why it matters
- Back-loading is useful for teams with short-term cap pressure but still has a floor in year one.

documents.title: Front-Loaded Split
documents.summary: The contract stays compliant because the total matches the TCV and the deal is clearly front-loaded.
documents.body_md:
### Scenario
- Owner gives a player a three-year loaded contract with more salary in year one.

### Inputs
- Contract length: 3 years
- Target TCV: $90K
- Contract style: front-loaded

### Calculation
- Year 1 salary: $40K
- Year 2 salary: $30K
- Year 3 salary: $20K
- Total check: $40K + $30K + $20K = $90K

### Outcome
- The contract stays compliant because the total matches the TCV and the deal is clearly front-loaded.

### Why it matters
- Front-loading lets an owner spend more cap now to reduce future cap burden.

documents.title: Guaranteed Contract Cut
documents.summary: The owner absorbs a $56.25K penalty under the earned-salary schedule.
documents.body_md:
### Scenario
- Owner cuts a player before the full guarantee has been earned.

### Inputs
- TCV: $100K
- Guaranteed share: 75%
- Guarantee amount: $75K
- Earned amount at time of cut: $18.75K

### Calculation
- Cap penalty = guaranteed amount - earned amount
- Cap penalty = $75K - $18.75K = $56.25K

### Outcome
- The owner absorbs a $56.25K penalty under the earned-salary schedule.

### Why it matters
- This is the clearest example of why guarantees and cut timing matter in a salary-cap dynasty league.

documents.title: MYM Conversion
documents.summary: The player moves from a short-term one-year contract into a longer multi-year deal without changing the current-season salary.
documents.body_md:
### Scenario
- Owner converts a one-year in-season contract into a MYM deal at the same current salary.

### Inputs
- Current salary: $8K
- Current contract length: 1 year
- MYM term selected: 3 years total

### Calculation
- Current-year salary stays unchanged at $8K
- Remaining years inherit the allowed MYM structure from the submission preview
- New TCV is the sum of all years shown in the MYM preview

### Outcome
- The player moves from a short-term one-year contract into a longer multi-year deal without changing the current-season salary.

### Why it matters
- MYM is the main in-season tool for retaining waiver or auction players without forcing a same-day extension decision.

documents.summary: The contract becomes more back-loaded while staying above the year-one minimum threshold.
documents.body_md:

- Owner restructures a contract to change how salary is distributed across the remaining years.

### Inputs
- New target TCV: $75K
- Remaining years: 3
- Year 1 minimum rule: 20% of TCV

### Calculation
- Minimum year 1 salary: $15K
- Example restructure: $20K / $25K / $30K
- Total check: $20K + $25K + $30K = $75K

### Outcome
- The contract becomes more back-loaded while staying above the year-one minimum threshold.

### Why it matters
- Restructures let owners reshape cap timing without changing the total value owed.

documents.title: Salary Floor Via Front-Load
documents.summary: The owner can meet the floor without forcing a separate unwanted auction purchase.
documents.body_md:
### Scenario
- Owner needs to reach the salary floor by the end of the auction or contract deadline period.

### Inputs
- Team is below the floor by $12K
- Eligible player can be restructured or signed with a front-loaded split

### Calculation
- Increase year-one salary by $12K while keeping total contract value compliant
- Offset future years downward if needed to preserve the agreed TCV

### Outcome
- The owner can meet the floor without forcing a separate unwanted auction purchase.

### Why it matters
- This is one of the clearest practical reasons to use a loaded contract structure.

documents.title: Trade With Salary And Extension
documents.summary: The deal can process with both the salary adjustment and the extension if both are clearly stated and the teams remain cap compliant.
documents.body_md:
### Scenario
- Two owners agree to move a player, include traded salary, and attach an extension as part of the deal.

### Inputs
- Player salary this season: $14K
- Traded salary included: $4K
- Extension term: 1 year
- Comment requirement: extension terms must be documented in trade comments or proof of discussion

### Calculation
- Acquiring team net current salary hit: $10K after traded salary
- Future contract terms follow the approved extension preview

### Outcome
- The deal can process with both the salary adjustment and the extension if both are clearly stated and the teams remain cap compliant.

### Why it matters
- Trade War Room supports this workflow directly, so owners need a clear example of how salary and extension logic combine.

documents.title: Lineup Submission and Starter Governance
documents.summary: Owners must submit valid lineups before kickoff, respect the 3-starting-QB roster rule, and stay within active roster requirements.
documents.body_md:
### Lineup timing
- Lineups must be submitted before the kickoff of the first game each week.
- Players lock individually when their games start, so later players can still be moved until their own kickoff.

### Quarterback roster rule
- After the contract deadline date, teams can carry only three starting quarterbacks on the roster.
- Late-emerging taxi quarterbacks can create exceptions, but promoted taxi QBs can count against the cap.

### Roster integrity
- Owners still need to meet the active roster minimum and maximum rules described in Roster Management.

documents.title: Current Punter Scoring
documents.summary: A punter can produce meaningful points from both placement and average distance.
documents.body_md:
### Scenario
- Owner wants to understand how a punter earns points in the current setup.

- Punts inside the 20: 2
- Average punt yardage: 50.3

### Calculation
- PI points: 2 x 4 = 8
- ANY bonus for 50.00-59.99: 3
- Total from these events: 11 points

### Outcome
- A punter can produce meaningful points from both placement and average distance.

### Why it matters
- The league keeps punters as a real lineup position, so owners need clear math for weekly lineup decisions.

documents.title: Active Roster, IR, and Taxi Squad
documents.summary: How owners manage roster size, IR, and taxi squad eligibility across the season.
documents.body_md:
### Active Roster Size
- Minimum Active Roster Size:
- During the fantasy season, the minimum roster size is 27 players.
- After the Free Agent Auction, the minimum roster size is 27 players for the rest of the fantasy season.
- Maximum Active Roster Size:
- During the Free Agent Auction, the maximum roster size expands to 35 players.
- After the Contract Deadline Date, the maximum roster size is 30 players.

### Injured Reserve (IR)
- Following player designations are eligible placed on the to be placed on the league's IR with 50% automatic cap relief.
- NFL's Injured Reserve (IR or other forms of IR as designated by MFL),
- COVID-19 IR,
- Holdouts
- Suspended Players:
- For season-long or remainder of season suspensions occurring in the off-season, the contracts will not roll forward (owner choice). If they occur in-season, then the contract would roll forward, preserving the original process.
- Owner must decide if they’d like the contract to not roll forward prior to the contract deadline date. In this case it will be as though the contract does not exist. Salary will = o for that year and will reset back to it’s original salary prior to suspension after the fact.
- Important Notes:
- Players on IR do not count towards the active roster limit.
- Cap penalties may apply for non-compliance with IR rules. Refer to the Penalties section for more details.

### Taxi Squad
- Eligibility:
- Maximum of 10 players, with a minimum of 1 IDP (Individual Defensive Player).
- Only players selected in the league’s Rookie Draft, in the 2nd round or later, are eligible for the Taxi Squad for their first three years in the NFL.
- Demotion Deadline:
- Eligible players drafted in the Rookie Draft must be demoted to the Taxi Squad no later than the contract deadline date. Trades occurring within the season, the player must be demoted prior to that player’s NFL Team kicking off that week. (Caveat trade occurring just before kickoff, it may not be feasible for the demotion to occur on time. As such, any trade occurring on the day of kickoff we will grant leniency in demotion to the following week.)

source_refs.excerpt: How owners manage roster size, IR, and taxi squad eligibility across the season.

documents.title: Starting Lineup and Superflex Governance
documents.summary: Current lineup structure, lineup submission timing, and the 3-starting-QB roster restriction.
documents.body_md:
### Starting Lineup Requirements
- Minimum Starting Lineup:
- Each team must field a valid starting lineup each week, consisting of the following positions:
- 1 QB
- 2 RBs
- 2 WRs
- 1 TE
- 2 Flex (RB/WR/TE)
- 1 SuperFlex (QB/RB/WR/TE)
- 1 Kicker
- 1 Punter
- 2 DL (DE or DT)
- 2 LB
- 2 DB (CB or S)
- 1 Defensive Flex
- Lineup Submission:
- Lineups must be submitted before the kickoff of the first game each week. The platform (MyFantasyLeague) will automatically lock players in starting positions once their respective games begin.
- Lineup changes can be submitted up until the start of a player’s game. A player may not be substituted in or a player may not be substituted out once his game occurs.
- Non-Compliance:
- Failure to submit a valid starting lineup by the deadline will result in penalties, as outlined in the Penalties & Other Miscellaneous League Rules section.

### Super Flex Rules
- Maximum Number of Starting QBs:
- Beginning with the contract deadline date, all teams are only allowed to carry three (3) starting QBs on their roster at any time.
- This limit includes any QB on the active roster, as well as those promoted from the taxi squad who become starters.
- Exceptions:
- If a QB on the taxi squad becomes a starter after Week 1, they do not count against the 3 QB maximum.
- If a QB is promoted from the taxi squad during the season and becomes a starter, they will count against the 3 QB maximum, regardless of their Week 1 status.
- Monitoring Compliance:
- The league will monitor compliance using depth charts from sources like FantasyPros Depth Charts.
- If a team has more than 3 starting QBs on their roster, they must get in compliance by the kickoff of the following Thursday’s game. Non-compliance will result in the commissioner dropping the player that created the 4th QB on the roster.

documents.title: Expired Rookie Auction
documents.summary: How expiring rookie contracts move into the expired rookie auction workflow.
documents.body_md:
### Eligibility and Overview
- Eligibility:
- Any player with an expired rookie contract that is not extended prior to the deadline will become available via the “Expired Rookie Auction.”
- Auction Timing:
- The Expired Rookie Auction occurs during the first week in May.

### Auction Rules
- Nomination Process:
- There is a 2-3 day window to nominate a player for the auction.
- The starting bid must be $1K more than the player’s prior year salary.
- A link will be provided with the valid minimum salary for each player.
- Bidding Process:
- The bidding process mirrors the Free Agent Auction, with the exception that the high bidder must remain the highest for 36 hours to win the player, rather than 24 hours.
- If there is a change in the high bidder, the 36-hour timer resets.

### Contract Rules for Auction Winners
- Auction Contract Eligibility:
- Auction contracts follow the same rules as the Free Agent Auction, with contracts ranging from 1 to 3 years.
- If the contract is more than 1 year, it must be submitted no later than the contract deadline date in September.
- Player Retention:
- Players won in this auction are not eligible to be cut by any team prior to the Free Agent Auction.
- 7: Waivers

source_refs.excerpt: How expiring rookie contracts move into the expired rookie auction workflow.

documents.title: Free Agent Auction
documents.summary: How the summer auction starts, when the roster lock hits, and how the auction salary floor works.
documents.body_md:
### Auction Overview
- Auction Timing:
- The Free Agent Auction typically begins on the last weekend of July and lasts approximately one week.
- This is a mandatory league event, and compliance with auction rules is strictly monitored.
- Roster and Cap Requirements:
- Maximum Roster Size During Auction: 35 players.
- Minimum Roster Size by Completion of Auction: 27 players.
- Valid Lineup Requirement: You must be able to submit a valid starting lineup by the completion of the auction.
- Spending Flexibility:
- The auction system provides a dashboard indicating the maximum amount you can spend to fill your roster.
- For example, the system might show "Max you can spend to fill your roster is $20K," assuming you are filling all 35 roster spots. However, if you only need the minimum of 27 players, you may have additional spending flexibility, potentially increasing this amount (e.g., to $28K).
- It is the owner’s responsibility to avoid overbidding during the auction. The system prevents you from spending more than $300K, but it will not account for the minimum roster requirement.
- Bidding Process:
- The auction follows an "Ebay Proxy Style," where each bid needs to remain the highest for 36 hours to win the player.
- Owners are expected to nominate players within a 24-hour period. Missing a nomination incurs escalating penalties, starting at $3K and increasing with each offense.

### Auction Roster Lock Date
- 3 Days Prior to Free Agent Auction Start:
- This is the deadline for cutting players before the auction begins. After this date, rosters are locked until the auction is complete.

### Auction Floor
- Salary Cap Floor:
- The salary cap floor is set at $260K, which must be met by the completion of the auction or by the Contract Deadline Date.
- Maintaining the Cap Floor:
- Once the floor is reached, if a trade or IR designation occurs that results in a lower salary, the team is still considered compliant.
- Owners if eligible may restructure a contract and front-load it to meet this minimum.
- Objective:
- The goal is to ensure owners are engaged during the auction and not simply "sleepwalking" through it. Providing the option to front-load a contract offers an alternative to forcing teams to bid on players they don't want, while still requiring active roster management. Failing to adhere to this rule indicates a general failure by the owner to put their team in a position to succeed.
- Refer to the Penalties section of the rulebook for non-compliance issues.
- 6: Expired Rookie Auction

documents.title: Rookie Draft
documents.summary: How the rookie draft works, what each round means, and which picks are taxi eligible.
documents.body_md:
### Overview
- The Rookie Draft is the biggest event of the year and is mandatory for all owners.
- Any player who would be considered an NFL rookie is eligible for the draft.
- The draft is conducted live, and participation is expected either in person or via Discord.

### Draft Rules
- Round 1 Picks:
- Must be kept on the active roster.
- Rounds 2-6 Picks:
- Taxi squad eligible.
- Round 6 Picks:
- Cannot be traded and must be used to select an IDP player (including kickers and punters).

### Draft Order
- Rounds 1-5:
- The draft order for Rounds 1-5 is determined by the prior season's playoff results. Higher finishers in the playoffs will pick later, while lower finishers will pick earlier.
- Round 6:
- The draft order for Round 6 is determined via a random generator prior to the draft.

### Rookie Draft Salary & Contracts
- Contract Details:
- All rookies drafted in the league are initially signed to a 3-year contract.
- Salary in table above represents their yearly salary

### 1st Round Rookie Option (Effective 2025 Draft)
- Introduction of the 1st Round Rookie Option:
- Starting with the 2025 Draft Picks, all first-round picks will receive an "Option Year" in addition to the standard 3-year deal.
- Eligibility:
- The Option Year applies only to players selected in the first round of the Rookie Draft, starting with the 2025 draft class.
- Exercising the Option Year:
- The Option Year must be exercised before the contract deadline date of the player's final season under their rookie contract.
- Details:
- Full details, including salary increases and specific conditions, can be found in the Contract Transactions section [link to section].

documents.title: Waivers and Sunday FCFS
documents.summary: Blind-bid waivers, Sunday FCFS, and owner responsibilities after a successful claim.
documents.body_md:
### Overview
- Waiver System:
- The waiver system allows teams to acquire players who are not currently on any roster. There are two primary types of waivers used in the league: Blind Bid Waivers and First-Come, First-Serve (FCFS) Free Agency.

### Blind Bid Waivers
- Waiver Runs:
- Blind Bid Waivers run on Thursday, Friday, Saturday, and Sunday at 9 AM during the NFL season.
- Players are locked after each run until the next waiver period begins.
- Submitting Bids:
- Teams submit conditional blind bids where they can prioritize multiple waiver claims. The highest bid for each player wins the waiver claim.
- The blind bid winning amount becomes the player’s salary for the current season.
- Tiebreakers:
- If two teams submit identical bids for the same player, the tiebreaker process is as follows:
- All Play Record
- Overall Record
- Total Points
- Head-to-Head Record
- During the preseason and up until the completion of Week 1, a custom order based on the prior season’s final draft slot finish is used for tiebreakers.
- Bid Processing:
- The system processes bids top to bottom within each group and accepts the first valid bid, ignoring the rest in that group.
- The groups are processed without priority over one another, ensuring all bids are evaluated equally.

### First-Come, First-Serve (FCFS) Free Agency
- FCFS Availability:
- After the Sunday morning waiver run, free agency opens on a first-come, first-serve basis.
- Players can be picked up freely until the kickoff of their respective games.
- Player Salary:
- Any player acquired via FCFS will have a $1K salary amount for the current season.
- The default contract length for FCFS acquisitions is 1 year.
- Purpose:
- FCFS ensures that teams can always field a starting lineup, even if they missed a player during the waiver runs.

### Waiver Compliance and Responsibilities
- Roster Compliance:
- Owners are responsible for ensuring their roster complies with league rules after making waiver acquisitions.
- Teams must have enough roster space and cap room to accommodate players acquired through waivers.
- Contract Terms:
- The default number of years for a waiver and FCFS acquisition is 1 year unless otherwise stated.
- 8: Trades

source_refs.excerpt: Blind-bid waivers, Sunday FCFS, and owner responsibilities after a successful claim.

documents.title: Trades and Compliance
documents.summary: Trade eligibility, trade review, trade comments, and post-trade roster and contract compliance.
documents.body_md:
### Overview
- Trading Period:
- Trading is allowed during the entire offseason and up until the NFL's Thanksgiving week, which marks the league's trade deadline.
- After the trade deadline, no trades are permitted until the start of the offseason.

### Eligibility
- Eligible Players:
- Any player with 1 or more years remaining on their contract is eligible to be traded.
- Expired rookies can be traded up until the extension deadline. Other expired contracts are not eligible to be traded.
- Draft Picks:
- All draft picks for the current draft year, as well as picks for 1 year into the future, are available for trade.
- Exception: Round 6 picks are not eligible for trade.
- Salary:
- Salary can also be included in trades. Please refer to the Cap Adjustments section for detailed rules on trading salary [link to Cap Adjustments section].

### Additional Trade Rules
- Compliance:
- All trades require both teams to immediately comply with roster standards. If a trade results in a team having fewer than the minimum required players for a valid starting lineup or total roster, the team must take immediate action to get back into compliance.
- If a team is out of compliance, the system will prevent them from submitting a valid lineup, which carries its own penalty.
- If a trade results in any contracts being out of compliance (such as exceeding contract limits or requiring adjustments), those changes must be made within 24 hours. Failure to correct contract discrepancies within this timeframe could result in penalties.
- Trade Assets Requirement:
- Teams are required to include at least some asset (not salary) in every trade. This rule was implemented to avoid asset-less trades, which caused dissatisfaction among league members.
- This rule ensures that even during the auction, trades include tangible assets, preventing awkward transactions where players are simply dumped due to roster or budget constraints.
- Trade Review Process:
- All trades are processed immediately to ensure timely processing.
- All trades are subject to review to ensure they fall within guidelines and may be subject to reversal.
- Extensions and Salary Compliance:
- If a player involved in a trade is eligible for an extension, the team trading the player away can agree to apply the extension as part of the trade. This must be stated in the trade comments, or proof of discussion must be provided before the trade is approved.
- All teams involved in the trade must ensure salary compliance according to the league's salary cap rules.
- 9: Contracts

documents.title: League Finance
documents.summary: League dues, payment timing, payouts, and fee handling.
documents.body_md:
### Overview
- League Dues:
- Each team is required to pay an annual league fee of $200. This fee covers the league's operating expenses, including the MFL hosting fee in addition to what is entered into the payout pool.

### Payment Schedule
- Dues Payment Deadlines:
- Half of the league dues are owed by the start of the Free Agent Auction.
- The remaining balance is due by the trading deadline, which falls on Thanksgiving.
- Late Payments:
- Teams that fail to make payments on time will incur a $3,000 cap penalty for each week the payment is late.
- All penalties accrued up until the contract deadline date apply to the current season. Any penalties accrued after this date are applied to the following season.

### League Payout Structure
- Payouts for the Season:
- MFL Fee: $100 (covers league hosting)
- League Champion: $900
- League Runner-Up: $450
- 3rd Place: $150
- Division Winners: $50 each (Total: $200)
- Weekly High Score (Weeks 1-17): $30 per week (Total: $510)
- Hawktuah Bowl Winner: $90
- Adjustments:
- ‘2024 MFL Fee has been increased from $90 to $100, with $10 subtracted from the Top Points Scorer in the Hawktuah Bowl.

### Miscellaneous Fees
- Logo Changes:
- Teams wishing to change their team logo will incur a $15 charge to cover the cost of a new owner banner.

### Payment Methods
- Payment Platform:
- All league dues and fees are to be paid via Venmo to @Keith-Creelman.
- 11: Penalties and Miscellaneous League Rules

source_refs.excerpt: League dues, payment timing, payouts, and fee handling.

documents.title: Penalties and Miscellaneous Rules
documents.summary: Penalty placeholders, cap-floor language, and miscellaneous rules like retired-player handling.
documents.body_md:
### Overview of Penalties
- Penalties are applied to maintain the competitive balance and integrity of the league. All owners are expected to adhere to the league rules and deadlines to avoid incurring penalties.
- CURRENTLY THIS SECTION WILL BE UNDER REVIEW BY THE COMMISH FOR AN OVERHAUL TO MAKE THIS MORE CONSISTENT ACROSS THE LEAGUE.

### Placeholder for Penalties
- Late Payment Penalties:
- Teams that fail to make dues payments on time will incur a $3,000 cap penalty for each week the payment is late. All penalties accrued up until the contract deadline date apply to the current season. Penalties accrued after this date are applied to the following season.
- Failure to Meet Salary Cap Floor:
- If a team fails to meet the salary cap floor by the completion of the auction or by the Contract Deadline Date, an immediate cap hit will be applied to bring the team into compliance. Additional penalties may be assessed as specified in the penalty section.
- Overbidding During the Auction:
- If a team overbids during the Free Agent Auction, and the system prevents them from submitting a valid lineup, the team will be required to restructure contracts to regain compliance. If the owner has already used their maximum allotment of restructures, further penalties will apply. Details will be provided in this section.
- Roster and Contract Compliance Post-Trade:
- If a trade results in contracts being out of compliance, adjustments must be made within 24 hours to avoid penalties.
- Waiver Wire Cap Penalties:
- Penalties may apply if a player acquired via waivers is later dropped before the Contract Deadline Date. The specific penalties related to waiver wire drops will be detailed here.
- Non-Compliance with Roster Requirements:
- Teams failing to maintain the minimum or maximum roster size by specified deadlines will face cap penalties. The specifics will be detailed here.
- Miscellaneous Penalties:
- Any other penalties that may be applied for various infractions will be captured here. This could include penalties for failing to meet other league requirements, such as participation in mandatory events.

### Placeholder for Penalty Details
- Penalty Amounts and Application:
- This section will detail the specific amounts for each type of penalty and how they are applied.
- Appeal Process:
- This section will outline the process for appealing penalties, including the timeline for submitting an appeal and how the commissioner and league members will review it.

### Miscellaneous League Rules
- Jail Bird Rule:
- Players whose playing careers are derailed due to legal cases resulting in release from their NFL teams are eligible for cap-free cuts.
- Retired Players Rule:
- Retired players can also be released cap-free. However, if a manager chooses to keep a retired player on their roster, the player’s contract will remain unchanged with no cap relief.
- Compensation for Retired “Tier 1” Players (Calvin Johnson Rule):
- Specific compensation rules apply to retired players who were categorized as “Tier 1” based on their performance and contribution to their teams. This section will detail the rules and compensation structure.
- Miscellaneous Adjustments:
- Any other rules that don’t fit into traditional categories or that are unique to certain situations will be captured here

documents.title: Topic History: League Overview
documents.summary: 2014 rules define 2 conferences with 2 divisions of 3 teams; the current rulebook uses 4 divisions (no conferences).
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- 2014 rules define 2 conferences with 2 divisions of 3 teams; the current rulebook uses 4 divisions (no conferences).
- 2014 entry fee was $50 ($60 for new owners); current rulebook lists $200 dues.
- Competition Committee (CC) was eliminated (during the season the Baldarellis were banned); current governance is commissioner-led.
- Current rulebook adds admission quiz, two-season probation, and dispersal draft for new owners, which are not present in legacy versions.
- 2012 poll adopted a bench-player (non-QB) tiebreaker for playoff/regular-season ties; this rule was removed prior to the 2025 season by commissioner ruling.

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- 2014: League hosted on MFL; 12-team salary cap league.
- 2014: 2 conferences with 2 divisions of 3 teams each; realignment every 3 years based on all-play record Weeks 1-13, with specific division groupings and .500 all-play assigned to new owners in realignment years.
- 2014: Regular season Weeks 1-13; playoffs Weeks 14-16.
- 2014: Competition Committee (Keith Creelman, Ryan Bousquet, Eric Mannila).
- 2014: Schedule uses double-headers each week (26-game regular season).
- 2014: Standings/tiebreakers defined for divisional champs, wild cards, and 1 vs 2 seeds (Pct, Div Pct, H2H, PF, All-Play, PWR, coin flip order).
- 2014: Championship/Toilet Bowl seeding and draft-pick implications are conference-based (details in 2014 rulebook).

### Unresolved Follow-Ups
- Confirm whether legacy voting thresholds (51% off-season, 90% in-season, 75% dues) still apply under commissioner-led governance.
- Confirm whether conference-based standings/tiebreakers and double-header scheduling still apply (detailed in 2014 rulebook but not in current).
- Pin down the exact season the bench-player tiebreaker was removed (known to be pre-2025).

documents.title: Topic History: League Calendar
documents.summary: Season rollover moved from Feb 1 to March 1 starting with the 2014 season; later shifted to March 1-15 (2018 and current).
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- Season rollover moved from Feb 1 to March 1 starting with the 2014 season; later shifted to March 1-15 (2018 and current).
- Rookie Draft timing shifted from June (2014) to mid-June (2018) to Memorial Day weekend (current).
- Free Agent Auction timing shifted from Aug 1 (2014/2018) to the last weekend of July with a 3-day prior roster lock (current).
- Trade deadline moved from Week 12 (2014) and Week 11 (2013/2014 by-laws) to Thanksgiving kickoff (2018/current).
- Playoffs moved from Weeks 14-16 (2014/2018) to starting Week 15 for 3 weeks (current).
- 2018 explicitly abolished the rule that playoff-period acquisitions are ineligible to be retained; current rulebook does not mention that rule.

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- 2014: Off-season rule-change voting thresholds: 51% for future changes; 90% for in-season changes; non-responses excluded; CC may act unanimously.

### Unresolved Follow-Ups
- Tagging period and deadlines are not listed in the current calendar; confirm if franchise/transition tagging still exists and when it occurs.
- Confirm the April 30 expired-rookie extension deadline supersedes older June/July dates.

documents.title: Topic History: Roster Management
documents.summary: Roster size increased from 23-26 (2014) to min 27 and max 30 after the contract deadline (max 35 during auction) in the current rulebook.
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- Roster size increased from 23-26 (2014) to min 27 and max 30 after the contract deadline (max 35 during auction) in the current rulebook.
- Historical roster caps: 2012–2017 in-season max was 26, with auction-only overage up to 31; 2018+ in-season max is 30, with auction-only overage up to 35.
- Taxi rules changed: 2014 allowed max 9 with 5/3/1 contract-year tiers (forum vote 2014-02-11, 7–5); current uses a 10-player taxi with 1 IDP minimum and 2nd-round+ eligibility.
- Starting lineup shifted from the 2014 fixed structure to the current flex/superflex format with expanded IDP slots and a 3-starting-QB roster cap.
- Holdout/suspension handling moved from special-circumstance 25% relief options (2013/2014) to current IR rules (50% relief, rollover rules).
- 2012 poll rejected a minimum 8-IDP roster requirement (only lineup IDP requirements remained).
- 2014 forum vote (Feb 11, 2014) clarified taxi-squad cuts: players not on the active roster incur no cap charge when cut; 2nd+ round rookies cut before the contract deadline are penalty-free if never promoted; once promoted, cuts incur cap hits.

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- 2014: In-season roster max 26 (auction max 31), min 23; 24 hours to fix invalid lineup after transactions; must stay under $300,000 cap.
- 2014: Off-season (post-Week 16 through Aug 1) had no roster or cap limits.
- 2014 (forum vote 2014-02-11, 7–5): Taxi squad max 9 with contract-year tiers (5 with 3 years, 3 with 2 years, 1 with 1 year), 2nd-round+ eligibility, no return once activated, Sept 1 demotion deadline.
- 2014: IR had no limit; 50% salary reduction; activation required upon return; off-season IR eligibility enforced before auction.
- 2014 (forum vote 2014-02-11): No player cut while not on the active roster incurs a cap charge. Rookies drafted in Round 2+ cut before the contract deadline are penalty-free if never promoted; any player promoted from taxi and later cut incurs a cap hit.
- 2014: Starting lineup requirements were fixed (QB, RB, 2 WR, TE, 2 Flex, DL, LB, DB, 3 Def Flex, K, P).
- 2014: Lineup warnings for bye/out/doubtful starters; MFL auto-submits if lineup not set by Thursday.

### Unresolved Follow-Ups
- Confirm the season when suspended players became IR-eligible (owner confirmation says this was added later).
- Confirm whether the 2014 taxi squad limits by contract years still apply or are fully replaced by the current taxi rules.
- Confirm whether holdout relief is 50% (current) or legacy 25% with Week-8 options.
- Confirm whether the 2014 taxi-squad cut/guarantee rule (no cap hit unless promoted) remains the governing standard.
- 2014 forum proposal discussed adding a mandatory second RB (lineup 1QB/2RB/2WR/1TE/2Flex) potentially effective 2015; confirm if any version was adopted.

documents.title: Topic History: Rookie Draft
documents.summary: Rookie contract length was 2 years in 2012, moved to 3 years starting 2013 by league vote, then removed prior to the 2025 season by commissioner ruling for ease of management.
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- Rookie contract length was 2 years in 2012, moved to 3 years starting 2013 by league vote, then removed prior to the 2025 season by commissioner ruling for ease of management.
- Rookie salary schedule evolved: legacy docs specify 3rd+ round salaries ($2K or $1K); current rulebook only lists 1st/2nd round salaries in the table.

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- 2014: Rookie draft is 5 rounds, non-snake, 3-minute clock with ADP auto-pick.
- 2014: Draft order determined by playoff finish (toilet bowl picks 1-6; championship picks 7-12).
- 2014: Supplemental draft uses blind bids with forfeited future picks; ties resolved by draft order.

### Unresolved Follow-Ups
- Current rulebook does not specify rookie salaries for rounds 3+; confirm whether legacy $2K/$1K rules still apply.
- Confirm the current rookie-contract length enforcement after the pre-2025 removal (if any default length is still used).

documents.title: Topic History: Free Agent Auction
documents.summary: Auction format shifted from daily nominations (2014) and a 2-week auction (2018) to a 7-day minimum nomination window in late July (current).
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- Auction format shifted from daily nominations (2014) and a 2-week auction (2018) to a 7-day minimum nomination window in late July (current).
- Legacy docs list explicit warnings/fines for missed nominations; current rulebook does not define those penalties.
- Nomination cadence has been treated as flexible in practice (e.g., 2/day with a noon start and midnight reset, allowing multiple nominations within a 12-hour window).

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- 2014: Auction begins at midnight on Aug 1; no cuts allowed during auction.
- 2014: 1 nomination per day (CC may increase after 10 days); 24-hour timer resets on new bids.
- 2014: Bids in $1K increments, $1K min; roster must end 23-31 with full lineup; cap compliance required.
- 2014: Missed nomination warnings; once roster is full, missed nominations indicate owner is done bidding.

### Unresolved Follow-Ups
- Confirm whether missed-nomination warnings/fines from 2014 still apply.

documents.title: Topic History: Expired Rookie Auction
documents.summary: Expired rookie extension deadlines shifted from June 1 (2013/2014) and July 1 (2018) to April 30 (current).
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- Expired rookie extension deadlines shifted from June 1 (2013/2014) and July 1 (2018) to April 30 (current).

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- 2014: Expired rookie extensions handled before June 1; see legacy contract docs.

### Unresolved Follow-Ups
- Confirm whether the expired-rookie auction and extension deadlines are aligned with April 30 in the current doc.

documents.title: Topic History: Waivers
documents.summary: Blind-bid waiver run days changed from Wed/Thu/Sat/Sun (2014) to Thu/Fri/Sat/Sun (current).
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- Blind-bid waiver run days changed from Wed/Thu/Sat/Sun (2014) to Thu/Fri/Sat/Sun (current).
- Tie-breaker order changed from record-based (2014) to All-Play/Overall/Points/H2H (current).
- FCFS window expanded from Sunday 9 AM-1 PM (2014) to post-Sunday waiver run until kickoff (current).
- First blind-bid run timing has varied year to year (2012 poll selected first Wednesday after auction, but later seasons moved away from Wednesday runs).

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- 2014: Dropped players hit waivers Monday 9:00 PM; no waivers in off-season.
- 2014: Blind bid waivers run Wed/Thu/Sat/Sun at 9 AM; $1K min and $1K increments; tie-breaker order based on record/All-Play/points/H2H; pre-season tie-breaker is draft order.
- 2014: First waiver run is first Wednesday after auction; FCFS runs Sundays Week 1-16 from 9 AM (contract $1K).

### Unresolved Follow-Ups
- Confirm whether any rolling-waiver order or waiver-claim limits still exist alongside blind-bid waivers.

documents.title: Topic History: Trades
documents.summary: Trade votes were removed in 2013: commissioner processes trades; two collusion objections within 48 hours trigger a veto poll requiring 5 collusion votes.
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- Trade votes were removed in 2013: commissioner processes trades; two collusion objections within 48 hours trigger a veto poll requiring 5 collusion votes. Trades involving starters must process 4 hours before kickoff, and MFL blocks trades if any involved player has already kicked off.
- Trade processing later moved from commissioner with potential veto poll (2014) to immediate processing with post-review (current).
- Current rulebook adds an asset-required rule (salary-only trades prohibited) and disallows trading Round 6 picks.

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- 2014: Trades processed by commissioner; veto poll triggered if two owners raise collusion concerns.
- 2014: Trades allowed for current and one-year-ahead picks; trade window March 1 through Week 12 kickoff.
- 2014: Trades involving starters required 4-hour pre-kickoff processing; 24 hours to get under cap after trade.

### Unresolved Follow-Ups
- Confirm whether 2014 veto-poll mechanics are fully replaced by immediate processing with review.

documents.title: Topic History: Contract Management
documents.summary: Rookie contract length rule shifted from 2 years (2012) to 3 years (2013) and was removed prior to 2025 (see Rookie Draft section).
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- Rookie contract length rule shifted from 2 years (2012) to 3 years (2013) and was removed prior to 2025 (see Rookie Draft section).
- MYM (Mid-Year Multi) limit increased from 2 per season (2018) to 3 per season (current).
- Legacy docs include franchise/transition tag systems with compensation rules; current rulebook does not mention tags.
- Legacy docs use 20% cap-penalty on remaining salary; current uses 75% guarantee with monthly earning schedule.
- Legacy docs allow contract restructures (often capped at 2 per season); current mentions restructures but not limits.
- 2012 poll adopted off-season extension timing where raises apply only to extension years (not immediately).
- 2014 forum vote (Feb 11, 2014; passed 7–5) allowed restructures only when an extension is granted; extension cost can be incorporated into the current year and split evenly or front/back loaded.
- 2014 forum clarification (Jun 9, 2013) and vote outcome (Feb 11, 2014 tie) reaffirmed cap of six 3-year contracts, excluding rookie contracts.

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- 2014: Contract details were maintained in a separate Contract Guide (not included in the 2014 PDF).
- 2014 (forum vote 7–5 on 2014-02-11): Restructures allowed only when an extension is granted. Owners may incorporate the extension cost into the current year and may split the total charge evenly across remaining years or front/back load it; payout timing can change but total owed remains the same.
- 2014 (forum clarification 2013-06-09; vote tie on 2014-02-11): Max of six 3-year contracts on a roster; rookie contracts do not count toward the six.

### Unresolved Follow-Ups
- Confirm whether franchise/transition tags and compensation rules are still in effect (missing from current).
- Confirm restructure limits (e.g., max per season) and whether mid-season acquisitions can still be restructured.
- Confirm whether the 2014 extension-based restructure rule (passed 7–5 on Feb 11, 2014) is still the governing standard and whether any per-team limit applies.
- Confirm whether the six 3-year contract cap (rookies excluded) remains active and if any contract-year cap was ever adopted.
- 2013 forum debate questioned whether owners who acquire back-loaded contracts should face the same cap penalty when cutting (vs. relief for secondary owners); confirm the intended rule and whether any exceptions exist.

documents.title: Topic History: League Financing
documents.summary: Dues timing changed from Aug 1 deadline in 2014 to split payments due by auction and Thanksgiving (current).
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- Dues timing changed from Aug 1 deadline in 2014 to split payments due by auction and Thanksgiving (current).
- Payout structure appears updated for 2024 (current) versus 2014 amounts.

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- 2014: League dues $50 (returning) and $60 (new owners); payouts: Champion $255, 2nd $125, 3rd $50, Divisional champs $25 each.

### Unresolved Follow-Ups
- Confirm whether any legacy dues deadlines (e.g., Aug 1) still apply.

documents.title: Topic History: Penalties and Miscellaneous League Rules
documents.summary: Current penalties section is marked as under review with placeholders; legacy docs provide concrete warning and fine structures.
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- Current penalties section is marked as under review with placeholders; legacy docs provide concrete warning and fine structures.
- Legacy special-circumstance rules (holdouts, suspensions, jail, retirements) are more detailed than the current placeholders.
- 2012 Philip Rivers drop reversal established a precedent for reversing immediate drops when reported promptly; waiver-bid mistakes were still treated separately.

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- 2014: Warning/fine structure for lineup and nomination issues (2nd warning $10, 3rd +$20, 4th +$30 with lockout).
- 2014: Trade response strikes (4 days in-season, 2 weeks off-season); 3 strikes removes trading privileges.
- 2014: Replacement owners may cut 1 player penalty-free and are relieved of prior cap hits; multi-owner replacement uses dispersal draft rules.

### Unresolved Follow-Ups
- Penalty amounts and appeal process are placeholders; requires authoritative update.
- 2014 forum proposal to run MFL pools (Survivor/Confidence/Pick Em) with a draft-pick prize (e.g., 1.13/2.13) was deferred on 2014-09-03; confirm whether any pool prize rules were later adopted.

documents.title: Topic History: Scoring Settings
documents.summary: Punter scoring changed after 2011: 2011 used gross punt yards (PNY) plus punts inside 20 at 2 points; starting in 2012 the league removed net-yards scoring and moved to punts inside 20 at 4 points plus punt-average bonus tiers (ANY).
documents.body_md:
### Change Notes
- Punter scoring changed after 2011: 2011 used gross punt yards (PNY) plus punts inside 20 at 2 points; starting in 2012 the league removed net-yards scoring and moved to punts inside 20 at 4 points plus punt-average bonus tiers (ANY).
- Return yardage scoring (UY/KY) begins in 2012 at 0.05 per punt return yard and 0.025 per kickoff return yard.

### Legacy Rules Not In Current Rulebook
- No legacy-only notes captured.

documents.title: TCV
documents.summary: Total Contract Value. The total value of a contract across all remaining years.
documents.body_md:
### Definition
- Total Contract Value. The total value of a contract across all remaining years.

### Owner Notes
- Contract length multiplied by the salary structure across the remaining years.
- Extensions and restructures reset TCV to the remaining years after the move.

source_refs.excerpt: Total Contract Value. The total value of a contract across all remaining years.

documents.title: AAV
documents.summary: Average Annual Value. The evenly distributed yearly baseline used when evaluating extensions and loaded splits.
documents.body_md:
### Definition
- Average Annual Value. The evenly distributed yearly baseline used when evaluating extensions and loaded splits.

### Owner Notes
- AAV is the baseline comparison point for front-loaded and back-loaded structures.
- The UPS extension schedule adds new salary relative to the player’s current-year AAV context.

source_refs.excerpt: Average Annual Value. The evenly distributed yearly baseline used when evaluating extensions and loaded splits.

documents.summary: Live MFL 2026 settings show rosterSize 50, while the written rulebook still uses active-roster minimum and maximum language centered on 27, 30, and 35.
documents.body_md:
### Conflicting statements
- Live MFL 2026 platform setting: rosterSize = 50.
- Written rulebook: minimum active roster size is 27, maximum is 35 during the auction and 30 after the contract deadline.

### Why this remains open
- The live platform appears to describe total stored roster capacity, while the written rulebook describes active-roster policy. The two should not be flattened into one number without commissioner confirmation.

### Recommended current handling
- Display both numbers separately, use live MFL settings for platform behavior, and use the written 27/30/35 language as the active-roster policy until resolved.
documents.needs_confirmation_reason: MFL total roster capacity and written active-roster policy are not expressed the same way.

comparison_cards.label: Roster Size Conflict

Offense.short_description: Number of Blocked Field Goal TDs
Offense.detail_description: This is the number of blocked field goals that are returned all the way for a TD. The player that blocked the field goal is not necessarily the same player that returns it for a TD. So one player could have a blocked field goal and another player could have a blocked field goal TD on the same play.

This is the number of blocked punts that are returned all the way for a TD. The player that blocked the punt is not necessarily the same player that returns it for a TD. So one player could have a blocked punt and another player could have a blocked punt TD on the same play.

Offense.short_description: Number of Fumbles Lost Returned for a TD
Offense.detail_description: This is the number of fumbles lost by a player in a game that are returned all the way back for a TD by the other team. For example, if a running back fumbles the ball and it ends up being recovered by the opponent and returned for a TD on the same play. This includes fumbles that are lost on offense or on special teams.
